explicit_text: Buck Duke was a powerful figure connected to the tobacco industry and the cotton mills. I knew people who worked for him, like Reuben Rink, who was his salesman sent worldwide. Buck Duke was part of the Duke family empire that controlled much of the business in Durham and beyond, including the American Tobacco Company and Duke Power Company.

explicit_text: Bill Erwin was the man who headed the Erwin Cotton Mills. I knew his daughter Margaret, who was quite snooty and thought highly of herself, though she eventually married a policeman in Charlotte. I had been inside the Erwin mansion but never had any real relationship or admiration for Bill Erwin himself, as he was part of the Duke cotton mill power structure.

explicit_text: Linotype was a new invention at the time, and my father bought one for ten thousand dollars, which was a big investment. I learned to operate the linotype machine and practiced on a dummy keyboard at night. It was interesting work, and I was put to work on it by the man who sold it to us from Brooklyn.

explicit_text: Old man Whittaker was the chief typesetter at our printery and a leader in the union movement. He was disgruntled and tired after years of hard work and wanted better wages and a union to protect workers. His son Henry raised rabbits, and I even bought rabbits from him to start a hutch. Whittaker was involved in a quarrel with me over the linotype machine and union issues.
